I would adhere to their requests and be done with it. I would see no reason to dwell on it.

i would talk calmly with my coworker and try to find a fair solution together. i would explain the situation and why it is important that one of us changes the schedule. if they still cannot change their plan, i would discuss for the team. i would do this because teamwork and communiction are important to keep the work running smoothly i would start working on the project as early as possible and manage my time well. this way,if my supervisor gives me additional tasks later, i will still have enough time to complete the project on schedule. i would stay calm and discuss the feedback with my manager to understand they perspective. i would explain my view politey and ask how i can improve to avoid negative consequences

I would negotiate a different week off and maybe important dates too e.g. I get christmas and they get NYE. I'd ask my boss if there were any tasks that required completion or prioritising, whilst also adhering to my two week deadline for my project. I would ask for a meeting to discuss the feedback and circumstances around it. I would be defending my work and work ethic with evidence but willing to listen to criticism if it's fair.

I would talk directly to the colleague to find out their vacation plans. If one of us is making plans that involve other relatives or a significant event, like an anniversary, then that vacation plan should take priority. This is especially true in situations that involve custody arrangements with minor children where vacation opportunities and rescheduling are limited. I would make an effort to get the project done early and let my boss know when it's completed so I'm available to take on extra tasks. I probably wouldn't do anything as far as reporting it because trying to undermine a manager by going around or above them affects the chain of command and trust. I would make an effort to communicate directly with the manager if I felt there were extenuating circumstances to the feedback that were not originally understood.

I will make the necessary changes so that we can both go on vacation at a different time while not leaving the company understaffed. I would get the project done early and give myself plenty of time to finish it. Even though my workload is light now, I don't know what's going to come up between now and then. I would listen to the feedback regardless of how I feel about it. All that matters is doing things the way I am told to do them.

I can change mine, no problem. I haven't bought tickets yet and I'm flexible. If i really needed the same week, I can ask my supervisor who asked first. That person should have priority. I would do the best i can, given the constraints. sometimes i have the flxibility to stay a little later and put in extra hours. or if needed i can ask for help to get everything done on time. i would ask them to explain it to me so that i understand, and then if i disagree still i would tell them so., other than that i wouldn't worry about it too much.

I would approach them to work on a compromise. I understand vacations are important but realistically they should have booked the time off before making plans. However since we have to work along I'd rather find an amicable agreement - maybe they can give me some time off they have booked in return. I would get as much done as I can now in order to have free time between now and when new tasks might arrive. If I finish quickly I can also assist teammates with their workload until things pick up for me, rather than coasting until the due date. I would ask for written clarification on their feedback, and speak to my HR representative about it. I would consider engaging an employment lawyer if my income was being threatened by someone in a malicious manner.

In order to keep the peace, I would reschedule my vacation. I would do this since it's clear the other person is not willing to budget, and there is work that needs to be done. Since my workload is light leading up to the due date, I would try to work on the project during this freed up time so t hat in the event my boss gives me last minute tasks t hat can take significant amounts of time and attention, I don't have to worry about not having the project due in time. I would definitely counter their point if I believed that it was not true since it could carry real consequences like loosing my annual bonus. If I have proof to back up my claim, i would also put that all together.

If my vacation was due to an unchangeable important event I would tell my boss I will not be in that week as I have something I can't miss.  Otherwise I would change my week I would tackle it right away and be done with it.  Then I am free and don't have to worry about it, especially if the boss makes changes or adds to it, I will have time to adjust I would grit my teeth and play their game, because if I was to argue or resist, it would only make the situation worse.  I know that the feedback is wrong, and my coworkers as well.

I would be considerate of them and their time and try to adjust my own vacation plans that way to not inconvenience my co-worker or the company. I would try to make sure that the following week I would be permitted my vacation and try to adjust my bookings to match the dates. I would ask a lot of follow up questions to my boss and the people around me if there is anything I am uncertain of. I would do my best to handle this task efficiently and effectively. I would ask many questions on how I can improve my work performance and if there is anything that needs immediate attention within my work. I would also try to not take it to heart and think of the ways that I can come back even better.

My initial response would be determined by any wider context. In any instance i would approach with professionalism and courtesy. If my request had been submitted prior to my colleague's, and I had received approval etc, then I would politely approach my supervisor and explain that as I had previously made my request and it was approved, that I had made plans and an assumption that it was all ok. I would also expect my supervisor to manage holiday requests in a fair way. 

If however both requests had been submitted at the same time, and no approval had been given yet, then I would look to support the team if possible, and if the dates of my request could be changed, then i would look to do so and also be hopeful that this would be considered if similar happens in the future. I would look to get the bulk of the project done as early as possible, and not bank on being able to do it at the last minute. I would then review it again closer to the deadline to see if it needed to be tweaked, however as my workload is light, i would look to have it at a completed status that i am happy with and allow a buffer for if any late requests come in. I would feel slightly aggrieved, but approach it in a polite and civil way. I would explain why i feel it may be incorrect and ask if there was any data or evidence to support the feedback either way. If the feedback is correct, i would accept this if it is justified. If it is clearly wrong and evidence suggests otherwise, i would politely challenge this.

I would offer to change my vacation plans if possible and ask if their is flexibility because I would want to be a team player and support my job. I would break it up into chunks and work on it piece by piece throughout the two weeks to make sure I got the project done as well as keeping the work load light and manageable. I would ask for a second opinion or call in for support from other coworkers.  I would listen and ask respectfully for proof or documentation before I moved forward.

I would be accommodating and let the colleague take that week so we can be on good terms for the rest of our assignments and projects. I would finish my project early in order to make room for a potential extra task from my boss, just so I can accommodate for his requests. I would thank the manager for the feedback and reassure them that I will work to improve on the areas that need growth.

I would yield to my coworker. If I have to change my plans, I change my plans. At the end of the day we both want to take vacation and if I have the ability to alter mine when they can't I will. It's the right thing to do. I'm going to get everything done. If I have to pull all nighters to complete everything I absolutely will. At the end of the day if the work isn't finished then what am I being paid for? I'd go talk to my manager and have a constructive conversation. Instead of coming in negative and complaining, I'll try to talk it out about what I can do better. What can I improve on to make this situation never happen again or lost my benefits. Hopefully leaving the room with the manager feeling better about things as well as myself.

I would be flexible and switch my plans around so I wouldn't hurt anyone. I would see how I could be the one who makes changes so everyone else is happy. I would ask for assistance with the project so I could get it completed and ensure there was time to do anything else that was needed. I always use my time wisely and get things done. I would learn from the feedback and be grateful that my manager spent the time to assist me. I am always striving to improve so it's helpful for positive feedback so I can be the best.

I would see which one of us has deadlines that are closer and argue that a fair approach would be to allow the person with the later deadlines to go on vacation. This would ensure fairness between the two groups. I would make sure my project is almost done, so I do not feel stressed in the event that my boss might give me more work. I would ask my manager if we could meet to discuss further. I would then ask what they recommend I do to better myself and my situation at work.

I think it would depend on what my vacation plans look like. Did i have to pay money and book something? If yes - i would try and do and exchange with another co worker- this way it is a win win. Most companies allow someone to leave if they have coverage for their shift. I would most likely start my project early and do it in my spare time. That way if my boss gives me a last minute task i can put it on hold and start over when my free time comes back up. I think that i would try to fight it, especially if it is a negative consequence such as losing money. i would tr to reason with my manager - but i would come with receipts and go higher if necessary,

i woud accept that i need to work so say happy to delay my holiday for them and carry on like i dont care i would get my work done asap and then say i am free and offer to take on any extra work that is available i would thank them for the feedback and ask them to give advice on how you can improve and ask if we can catch up in a few week to make sure im on target etc

I'd probably change my vacation week to accommodate everyone. Though in the future i'd expect to keep my dates first. I'd knock out the project as soon as possible, freeing up my time for the upcoming future. I don't think putting it off is beneficial to anyone in this situation. I'd respectfully voice my concerns to my manager - if i feel a certain way i'm going to make it known. I think its completely valid to stand up for myself and my own feelings.

I would probably just take a different week off because I dont like dealing with stubborn people and we would probably just be talking in circles anyway without resolving anything I prefer to finish things as soon as I can because I dont like rushing around at the last minute. I would anticipate getting more work from my boss at any time so I would especially be more likely to try to finish the project right away I would be pretty unhappy about this and try to have a constructive conversation about it with my manager. I would be really worried about being affected by this and pretty irritated

I would willingly change my plans if I didn't have anything booked yet because I want to make sure I seem like I'm prioritizing the needs of my colleagues. I would probably still get the project finished quickly and take additional work because I want to seem like a team player. I would send them an email and kindly ask if I could get some additional feedback in a one-on-one because I want to improve.

I would have no decision in this matter, Id the other co-worker will not change their plans I will take my vacation in another week after the other co-worker returns, very simple. Be prepared for the last tasks, since he has given you these last tasks before, prepare for them just incase he indeed gives you these tasks to possibly finish them faster than normal, always good to be prepared. Immediately talk to my boss person to person, let them know I don't feel I am in the wrong and why and hopefully have evidence to back you up, I am a fighter but admit to my wrong doings, if I know I did nothing wrong, I will fight for it.

I would say, "no problem" and accept the day of work. It would look good and it would give the place an idea of what type of worker I am. It would also maybe lead to a favor owed. I would immediately get the project completed, in case my boss does exactly that. I would constantly keep that in mind, so that I could somewhat be mentally prepared. And if he did do it, I would give it my full attention to detail. I would ask to speak with him and request a detailed description of what it is that I did or didn't do. Depending on the attitude and my belief that it is not accurate, I would take it above his head if necessary. It would of course depend on my ability to prove that I didn't do this negative thing.

I would forgo my vacation plans and stay and work for the sake of the company and its customers. I've been on a forced vacation of sorts while looking for employment, and this job is much more important than taking a vacation. I would prioritize finishing the project ahead of schedule to best make use of the light work load and have time for any unforeseen tasks that may arise in the next two weeks. I would explain my side of the story in a calm and humble manner, but ultimately respect my superiors feedback if they were not swayed. I would feel bad about the situation, but would take the feedback to heart and try to make changes accordingly.

I would change my vacation plans to help the other colleague out with keeping their plans, I would just rearrange my vacation plans to another week. I would finish up the work that I have left to get that project complete and ask my boss if they need anything else done for this project, if they need things done at the last minute to complete the project, then I would have that time dedicated to those last minute tasks. I would ask to have a talk with the manager about the feedback and ask why I received this negative feedback so that I could learn and grow from it and make the changes that I need to make to turn this negative feedback into positive feedback.

I would do everything I could to change my vacation to allow my coworker the opportunity to take theirs. I can always take vacation another week and do not want to create any tension with my coworker. I would spend the majority of my time on the project, trying to finish it early. This would allow time for me to take on additional tasks if and when they come up, allowing me to keep up on all my tasks. I would ask to have more clarification on the feedback, explain why I feel differently in a respectful way. I would work to find a common ground and take any suggestions to improve moving forward. This would allow me to not create any tension with my manager.

Since I have been out of work for quite some time, I think I would just appease my colleague for this time. I don't think anxiety over my job is worth it is this colleague is determined to not change vacation plans. I would take advantage of things being "light" to really focus on the project since I know my boss likes to give me last second tasks. I would try to do as much as possible with the expectation that a last minute request might come up. It seems like in this situation there isn't much I could do. I could maybe go to HR but that would rock the boat more so unfortunately I'd have to bit my tongue.

I would put it to my supervisor to figure this scenario out. It is not within my role to determine vacation approval policies I would do as much work as possible ahead of time to front load everything and guarantee ive got enough time I would dispute their findings by asking for supporting evidence and documentation. Maybe id request another senior leader sit in on the conversation too

I would follow the same pattern as the last position, because it is centered on my individual foundation, not really the different situations. If I had plans I would push harder to keep my vacation, otherwise I would be flexible. I would continue working on the project at the pace I normally worked at. Even putting myself in the shoes of this different position, it wouldn't really change my work ethic of time management skills? I would feel hurt, and upset. It would probably ruin the rest of my day. I would still follow through with a formal response to cover my basis, and protect myself and my bonus.

I would change my vacation dates and allow my colleague the time off. This shows good team work and flexibility I would complete the project immediately with the understanding I can update prior to the due date if needed. This allows me time to complete any further tasks that may be allocated to me whilst still delivering the project, This shows good time management skills. I would ask the manager to talk me through the negative feedback and offer evidence that challenges this. I would also ask for feedback on what areas I need to work on and where the manager thinks I can improve

I would change my vacation plans because I would be a right thing to do because I was probably hired last so I would give the other colleague a chance to go on vacation. I would obey my boss and get the project done because I know that he trust me and has given me the responsibility to get the project done the right way regardless of it taking significant amounts of time and attention At first and negative feedback would make me feel bad, but I would make an offer to my boss to redo the work and get it right knowing that he had trusted me to do it right the first time

I would move my vacation to another week so that my coworker can have it off. This would help maintain a good work relationship with this individual and avoid any unnecessary strain on my supervisor. I would try to complete the project as soon as possible in case I am handed a last minute task by my boss. This way the project I was originally assigned is complete and I can still complete any last minute projects my boss may need me to jump in on. I would feel uncomfortable in this situation. However, I would respect their opinion and ask for a chance to explain myself and why I see it from a different perspective.

I Would Talk to Them and Advise Them and Inform Them That I Already Booked and Planned My Vacation Ahead Before I Put The Notice In and Try to Persuade Them But If They Don't Budge I Will Decide to Postpone My Vacation For a Bit. I Would Focus and Work Hard Give 100 Percent Of The Project and Make Sure It's Very Secured and Ready to Go Before the Due Date and Last Minute Then Be on Alert If I Get Any Extra Work. I Would Just Keep My Composure and Cool and I Wouldn't Even Worry About It, I Would Take it With a Grain of Salt, I Would Protect my Peace and Move On and Move Forward.

I would see if there is any one else that could possibly cover that week. If not, I would see if we can split the week. And if they could not compromise, I would let them have the vacation week but hope they would owe me in some way in the future. I would ask my boss ahead of time if there are any tasks they need me to complete ahead of time to give them a nudge that I need lead time to get the tasks done as well as the project. I would ignore it and move on. I don't think there is anything that can be done. Speaking to higher management would only make things worse.

I would let them take their vacation unless I absolutely can't change my time off. I would make sure I'm being as nice as possible and trying to let others get their time off if they need it. I would make a clear list and ask additional follow up questions. I would also make sure my boss was aware of the workload for it to properly be prepared in case any additional last minute tasks come up. I would feel hurt still but I would take it in stride. Obviously if they decided to tell me, it was necessary to address. I would make sure I fix what they believe was negative with my work and try my best to continue to do my job to the best of my ability.

I would change my vacation plans so that my colleague could take the time. I would do so in interest of the organization. I would complete the project immediately since I have a light workload and the ability to focus on it. I would schedule a follow-up meeting to restate  my point of view and demonstrate the quality of my work.

I would have to go around and see what I could do with my vacation. It is one of those situations where I would be unsure, but if I had to and it was really important, wow., I am not sure . I would set goals and times and things I should get done on certain dates in my planner. This would help me keep myself organized I would ask her what to do better, and let her know why I agree with it. It is better to say it outright, then keep it locked in, then you will explode.

I would talk to my colleague and ask that if they go on their preferred week of vacation, that I might be allowed to have preferential choice for the next major holiday (such as Christmas). That way,  it is fair. I would do the project as soon as I can. I don't know if other problems will come up that would make it difficult to complete the project. I can always use the extra time to perfect my work. I would attempt to address the communication issue between myself and my manager, so I can correct the issue without misunderstandings.

I would be willing to adjust my vacation dates if I was able to. I don't mind accommodating someone else if it does not affect me. I would make sure to get the project done as soon as possible, that way if another task is given to me, it can also be completed. I would discuss this issue with the manager and supervisor above them. I would politely stand up for myself and express how I do not agree and it is not true, and that I should not face consequences for something I did not do.

I would always prioritise the needs of the children our organisation looks after. I would apply to change the date of my vacation to ensure that the needs of the children come first. I would contact my boss to see if I could be given some tasks in advance. I would explain that my current workload is light and I would not be neglecting any priorities if I were to take on additional work now, as opposed to right before the deadline. Alternatively, I would ask if there was any opportunity for me to receive some additional help in the week of the deadline. I would arrange to meet with my manager to discuss the feedback. I would explain that I always value constructive criticism, as it helps me identify where I can improve. However, feedback that is inaccurate does not provide me the opportunity to improve.

I would agree to change my plans to another week in order to get along and show a willingness to compromise. I would finish the project early so that I would still have plenty of time to finish last tasks that the boss gives me. I would apologize and promise to improve but I would respectfully give my opinion as to why the feedback was flawed.

I would gladly change my plans and let the other person have the week. It would prevent any problems. I would work on the project in my spare time and have it ready before it was due. That way if I was given additional work I wouldn't have to worry about getting it done. I would request a talk with my manager and try to find out why he/she left the negative feedback. I would do anything I could to make sure there were no lies told about me.

It depends on whether I had already paid for reservations or tickets or something like that. If I was just arbitrarily choosing a week to go on I would get it done sooner than later, so if my boss assigned any surprise work, I would have time to do it without feeling pressured. I would respectfully explain why I felt it was unfair to me to hold me responsible for something I felt wasn't my fault. I would make my case respectfully but firmly.

I would because usually I do not have anything else blocking such a change anyway. I am flexible in most situations such as this one. I would continue to handle it as normal since I have done similar projects with success in the past. I would ask for a review from another higher up. I would also look at the performance as an opportunity to improve as long as my investigation reveals it was warrented.

I would let my coworker take the week off.  I feel for their situation and i am a team player who puts the company over myself I would work as hard as possible to get the project done so that i am ready for any extra tasks that my boss might give me. i would keep my mouth shut.  i want the team to operate without drama so i would take this on the chin

I would look at how flexible my vacation plans are, and try to understand where my coworker might be coming from. Maybe they have family they need to visit, or a different urgent situation that they need to attend to. If I am able to move my dates, I absolutely would to help accommodate the needs of my coworker, especially if it is not of great concern to me. I would try to get my project done as soon as possible to make sure that I can accommodate my boss' needs, especially if they are closer to the due date. I would set an early due date for myself to ensure that I would have ample time to complete everything, as well as review my project to make sure it's the best project that I could have completed. I would take the manager's feedback into consideration, and try to understand where they are coming from. I would make sure to book a meeting with my manager to discuss how exactly they feel that this negative feedback is manifesting in my work, and see if I can understand why there may have been a miscommunication. I would try to share my side of the story, my perspective so that they can wholly understand the situation. That being said, I would also take this feedback and see if I can change my practice if it's warranted.

I would have a discussion with my coworker to see if there was some kind of compromise that we could come to. Maybe we could split the week in half or they can cover me during an upcoming week where it is busy. I would get the project done early, ensuring the care of the children would not be impacted and my colleagues would not endure any additional stress. To do this, I may have to divide the work up over several days, but I'd try to get it done in the first week to allow for any last minute tasks in the second week. I would feel bad - if my performance is not up-to-par, that would affect the kids and also my colleagues, who need to be supported in order to provide quality care. I would try my best to improve my performance as soon as possible and try to check in with my supervisor regularly to ensure that I was making improvement.

I would change my requested week of vacation. I would accomodate my co-worker and let them have their way. I would brace myself for last minute work being stacked on me. I would have that expectation because that is the evidence for how my boss behaves towards me in the workplace. I would provide data and evidence to the contrary. I would appeal the feedback. I would ask for a second evaluation, perhaps from a different manager.

i would have a calm conversation with my colleague to understand why that week matters to them and see if we can work out a fair solution. In childcare, it is important that the team is properly staffed so the children can receive consistent care. If we can't agree, I would ask the supervisor to help decide. my priority would be making sure the children and team are supported. I would start the project early so i have plenty of time to complete it carefully. This also leaves room if my supervisor needs help with other things. In a childcare setting unexpected tasks can come up, so being prepared is important. I might feel surprised, but i would feel open to the feedback and remain professional. I would ask my manager for examples so I could understand things better and learn from the feedback. Even if i do not fully agree, i would reflect on the feedback and look for ways to improve. My goal would be to grow and provide the best care possible.

I would go ahead and let the coworker take vacation on my week and I'll reschedule for another week. I would go to my boss and ask it there are any other tasks that he needs help with to maximize my contribution to handling the office workload. I would ask my boss for suggestions of what I can do differently in his opinion.  I would not share my opinion at that time, but would just try to navigate around what he thinks I'm doing wrong so I can fix it.

I would be willing to shift my vacation time as long as I did not have any prepaid plans and it was not going to be for a family event or something similar. I would also speak with my coworker about the issue and see if they had any future vacation plans that might coincide with mine. I would work on the project as much as possible so that I could finish it before the due date, and have time to review and make any corrections or updates to it prior to turn in. Since you never really know what's going to happen tomorrow, there's no sense putting off something that could be done today. I would speak with my manager and find out where they got their information from, and what would lead them to believe I was solely responsible for the issue. If the manager was unwilling to listen or to even hear my side of the issue, I would take it up to the next level and/or HR if necessary.

In this situation, I would change my schedule to help the team. Though it would be difficult, I feel like a change of vacation will help make everyone happy and help serve our clients I would focus on completing the project as soon as possible to avoid getting caught up with delays and give me and my boss enough time to review the project and deal with unforseen cirumstances I would feel very disappointed but I would try my best to meet with my manager to discuss how it can be rectified and mitigate chances of bad consequences.

In this scenario, I would change my plans so that my colleague can take their vacation and we can ensure the workplace is adequately manned. I would be willing to compromise for the sake of the workplace. This would be the easiest course for a peaceful resolution. I would take personal time outside of work/business hours to complete this project. I don't want my performance during the workday to begin to fall. I would demonstrate that I can be proficient and productive, even with the heavy workload and pending project. In this situation, I would feel disappointed in myself and resentful towards my manager, but I would learn to accept it and improve so that I can project a better image of myself. I would use this as an opportunity to show my manager I can perform better and meet their standards.

I would meet personally with my colleague who is unwilling to change their vacation plans to see if we might be able to come to a compromise. If they are unable to do so (ie. already purchased plane tickets, hotels, family arrangements, etc.) then I would volunteer to move my vacation week. I would tackle the project head-on from the start, putting my all into completing it effectively and efficiently ahead of the due date so I may leave some time for any last-minute projects that come up. Before starting on my initial assignment, however, I would likely let my boss know that I will be working on this project and to let me know ahead of time should something else come up that needs my attention. I would consider the negative feedback on my own first and try and see things from my manager's perspective. I would work to implement the feedback as much as I could to comply with my manager and see if it does, indeed, make sense. However, if I didn't find the feedback was effective after a period of time, I would meet with my manager, voice my concerns, and ask for guidance on how to incorporate it into my workflow in the way they pictured.

I would approach my colleague and try  to see if we could find a compromise that works. I would explain the situation and see whether there might be any flexibility in their plans.
 I would begin working on the project early and create a simple plan.  starting early would allow me to make steady progress and ensure the project is completed.
 I would feel concerned. I would ask my manager for specific examples and clarification I would take it as an opportunity to better understand my manager’s expectations.

I think it would depend on who requested the vacation first. If I requested it first, I would absolutely not change my plans unless I was given additional compensation for doing that, because I think that would be fair. If I didn't request it first, I would be much more willing to be flexible. I would get ahead of the game. Work is by nature unpredictable, so if I have a light workload right now, why wouldn't I take advantage of it and make progress on the project, so that I have the ability to handle any last minute tasks that may come my way later? I think I would try to talk through it with my manager and ask a lot of questions. Instead of just saying, "I don't agree", maybe something like "can you really break down XYZ"?" That would give me the ability to find the root of where we disagree and talk through where I see things differently, especially if I have evidence. It's really hard to respond to general conclusions.

I would offer to switch my vacation to a different week because it demonstrates empathy for the other colleague's situation and active cooperation within the company. I would try to work on as much of the light work as possible early to get it done with and I would request to meet with my boss to discuss any additional work that might need to be done ahead of the deadline, trying my best to cooperate but would also empathize and understand if those last minute tasks were beyond the control of my boss. I would feel very concerned and anxious about the situation. I would schedule a meeting with my boss to better understand their reasoning and justification and ask specifics regarding how I might improve my work performance in the future. I would explain my concerns along with any supporting evidence. If I still felt the justification was unfair, I'd consider arranging a similar meeting with HR or higher ups.

This would ultimately come down to who applied for the vacation slot first.  If my colleague applied first, then I would move my vacation regardless.  If I applied first, then it would depend whether or not my vacation is easily moved. I would focus as soon as possible to get a head start with the expectation that more duties are to come based on the past behaviour. I would explain the situation in a respectful way and seek further clarification.  Sometimes feedback may appear incorrect at first or on an emotional level so it is important to get clarification first.

I would talk to HR and see if something can be made to where both me and my colleague can take vacation on the same day. I would tell my boss that the current project is my main priority. Once I can successfully finish my project, then I can move on to last tasks. I would own up to it. Feedback is crucial in the workplace. I would talk to them and see what steps I can take to improve.

I would make it work.  I need to put my job as my number one priority and I need to be flexible to do that. I would plan ahead and get what I needed to do done early just in case this happens. I always assume that "last-minute" issues will arise, and plan my work accordingly to complete projects with plenty of buffer time. I would take a few days to sit with the feedback, and think about how it would feel to accept this feedback as true. If I still struggled to believe the veracity of the issue, I would talk to a trusted colleague, possibly from a previous job, or in another field, and ask them to share with me if they felt there was some truth to the feedback.

If after this self-reflection I still felt the information wasn't true, I would ask to sit with my manager and discuss the feedback again, asking for more specific examples. I might ask permission to offer a different perspective on whatever had led to the feedback, but I would work hard not to appear defensive or not open to criticism, and instead see it as a growth opportunity.

First, I'd evaluate two things: 1) Why my colleague is requesting vacation (i.e., is it urgent due to a family matter) and 2) When my colleague requested vacation. If it's due to a family matter, and my vacation request is for leisure, then I'd postpone my vacation to another time. In addition, if my colleague requested vacation earlier than I did, then I'd also postpone my vacation to another time. In both these instances, I might feel a bit upset that I couldn't take my vacation; however, I believe it's important to be empathetic towards others. On the other hand, if my colleague is requesting vacation due to leisure, and requested vacation later than I did, then I believe it's important for me to talk to them to compromise. This is especially important because I wouldn't want this happening between two other employees. If a compromise isn't able to be made, then I would involve my supervisor, coming from a place where I wouldn't want other employees to go through the same thing, as this could be stressful for them as well. First, I would break this project into multiple parts and assign them to multiple days, ensuring I cover all my basis and don't leave anything until last minute. In addition, considering my boss gives me last tasks that can take significant amounts of time and attention, I would schedule a private meeting with them to ask them if there's any tasks they'd like me to complete in the next 2 weeks. My main goal here is to complete my project as efficiently and effectively as possible, without missing steps, in addition to ensuring I have enough time for these last tasks. Finally, if my boss keeps giving my last tasks, I believe it's important to address this, such as talking to my boss about assigning tasks at least a week in advanced so everyone, including myself, has the ability and capacity to complete it accurately, as people whom rush tasks might make mistakes. In terms of how I feel, I'd be somewhat upset. However, I'd reflect on whether or not this feedback is objectively true, such as asking other people I work with. This is because I believe that we might not be cognizant of our mistakes, and it's important to be honest with ourselves to improve. After this, I would set up a private meeting with my manager regarding their feedback, asking for specific examples. If I truly don't believe that it's true, I would offer my point of view in a curious and not hostile matter. Regardless of what happens, I would want to change because my manager must have some sort of evidence if they're providing me that feedback, and I'd work with my manager to come to a consensus on what I can do to improve. At work, I believe that everyone can improve, even if they might be the best employee.

So yes I would be willing to change when my vacation as long as it doesn't occur any negative outcome for me. I would be diligent in getting my project up to speed quickly well before the due date in two weeks. This allows for time to be dedicated to any last minute tasks given to me by my boss. I would still take the feedback and see if there are ways to improve in the issues they deemed to be negative. It might also be worthwhile for me to have another talk with my manager about how I view this feedback and might see some arguments against it. But that I am willing to put in the word needed to gain a more satisfactory standing.

I would be more than willing to change and be flexible and make the adjustments to MY schedule to make sure there is proper coverage. I would first do most of the intricate work right away to make sure I had enough time to finish it, and any potential new tasks that may arise at the last minute. I would be unhappy with the evaluation, but would consider it constructive criticism and asks questions on how I can improve my abilities at work.

Well it depends on who put their time in first. If I put it in after them, it's only fair that I change my plans. Knowing my boss's habits, I would make sure that the project is done early. In the event my boss drops a load of work in my lap, I'll have time to work on his tasks without the project being delayed or incomplete. Request an additional meeting with my boss to clear it up. If I don't agree with it, it could be a misunderstanding that I could clear up with him or a client.

I value making sure that relationships are prioritized more than getting what I want so I would let the colleague take the vacation at the time they want and figure out how to move mine to a different time frame. I want to come across and agreeable and easy going. I would continue to work on the project so that if something happened with my boss giving me more work I would easily be able to tell them yes and maintain a good relationship with them. I would try to ask them about it privately but if they were insistent then I would just let it go. I wouldn't want to get into an argument or major confrontation over it because I do value their relationship at work.

If I agreed to change - as long as this didn't affect any other people outside work, otger relationshiops are important to me too - this would be obviously cooperative I would finish the work early and tell my boss that I had done so, so that they could give me more to do if they needed to I would want to discuss this further with my manager, and if necessary with a staff group. Someone senior is a last resort, it dpends what comes out as under threat

I would allow my colleague to take their vacation period and take mine at another time because this would paint me in a greater light and display selflessness. I would start the project as soon as possible so I am able to complete it efficiently and p[repare for any additional tasks that my boss may assign me. I would speak to my manager in a calm manner to discuss my disagreements, especially since the feedback carries consequences.

Since both my colleague and I can't be gone at the same time, I would change my vacation plans so that the business can keep running smoothly. I would make sure that I have the project done with plenty of time left to be able to do the last-minute tasks that my boss might spring on me. If my boss gave me negative feedback at work, I would make sure I go above and beyond to show them that I am a good worker.

If I did not have reservations or paid for any vacation plans and was able to change my vacation I would.  Otherwise I would have a discussion with this colleague. I would complete the project as soon as possible so i would have free time to handle the last minute tasks. I feel sad and disappointed about the situation. I would have a conversation with the manager.  I would ask why the negative feedback was given and explain my side.

I would try to compromise with my colleague. But if they are still unwilling to change, I would let them have the week. It is important that my job comes first. I would start working on this project immediately. It is unwise to wait until the due date if I have time now. This would be very upsetting to me. I would try to discuss the situation with my manager further and try to see where I can improve.

I believe that it would depend on what I had planned for my vacation time. If I had prebooked flights and accommodation that I could not get a refund for I would struggle with making changes. However if I was just going to be doing stuff around my house I would happily make adjustments to my time off. I would have a discussion with my coworker and see what we could come up with. I would start work on the project immediately, so that I knew that if anything else came up, I wouldn't not have to worry about starting and finishing the project before deadline. I would be very agree about the situation. First thing I would do is cry by myself, because that is what I do when I am super angry about something, but then I would make an appointment with my manager to discuss the situation and explain what actually took place, to try and save my job.

I would be willing to delay my vacation time in order to give my colleague the opportunity to take their vacation now. I would be conscious about my time and ensure that I can successfully complete all my necessary work by the due date. I would confront my manager about the negative feedback and ask for an opportunity to change their negative feelings toward my work performance.

I would take my vacation later to be on the bosses good side and make myself look better than my colleague while also making my colleague happy with the vacation time I would do all the work and complete it early so that I could also do the extra projects at the end without having to rush I would thank them and agree to work on whatever the feedback was. I would make sure they see me making the effort

I would rearrange my vacation plans so that work had enough coverage for the weeks I'd be away - if I cannot rearrange, make sure to find other cover for myself. I would make sure to start this project immediately, so that I have enough time to prepare and have it finished well before the due date. This way, if my boss gives me another task, I will have enough time for everything. I would calmly raise the concerns that I have (that I do not believe it is true) and give them the reasons I don't think it's true.

If I was able to move my vacation, then I would. However, if I had booked a non-refundable vacation or would lose a significant amount of money by moving or cancelling dates, I would talk to my co worker. If needed, we could have our supervisor mediate if we are unable to reach a compromise. I would start on the project as soon as possible. That way, if I am assigned other projects with shorter turn around and higher priority it would not impede my ability to get the project done. I would use any excess time to fine-tune and finish up all the details of the project. I would, politely, disagree with my supervisor and explain, in plain detail, where my supervisor and my views differ. If my supervisor still insists on their view, I would accept the correction.

I would change my vacation plans so that at least one of us would be at work. I can always take a vacation another time. I would use my free time this week to work on the project. I want to take advantage of the light workload I have, just in case something comes up less minute next week. I would feel upset that the feedback didn't meet my expectations. I would raise my concern with my manager and try to understand where the feedback is coming from, what I could have done differently, and what I need to improve on in the future.

I would move my plans but ask my supervisor to note this for the future. I am willing to get along but I want my sacrifice remembered so I do not have to next time. I would still try to finish the project with days to spare so I would have room to make up any other tasks. I would ask my supervisor to explain the feedback and perhaps request a meeting with them and their supervisor.

I would choose to move my vacation to a different week. It sounds like this week is very important to my coworker, and I can budge a little if it keeps everyone happy. I would get my project done as soon as possible so that, if my boss DOES have some last-minute tasks for me, I have time to get them done without getting stressed. And if they don't have any tasks for me, that gives me some breathing room. I wouldn't feel great about it, and I would ask my manager if we could talk about it one-on-one so I can see exactly what they see and make any improvements necessary.

I'd change my plans, the colleage seems to need this time off as they're not willing to change so I'd change my plans to be amenable. I'd get the project done immediately, that way if my colleague or my boss needed me for other tasks I'd be ready to support them. I'd call a meeting with my boss to discuss my feelings and problems with the review. If this didn't improve things I'd call a meeting with my boss to discuss with HR as a witness.

I would try and work it out to change my dates. If it didn't work I may try and negotiate dates to split up. I would want to try and maintain harmony I would get it done as soon as possible. I would want to be prepared to handle any extra added tasks I would ask what I did to deserve the feedback. I would schedule a meeting and find out how to move forward

I would try to see if my vacation can be rescheduled to an alternate week and if it can't, then the manager will have to make the call objectively. Although it would be good for colleague's to let them have the week, it should be fairly allocated. I would ask my boss ahead of time if there are any tasks due and let them know that I'll be devoting time for this project and may not be available closer to the deadline. This will show that I am on top of my things and thinking ahead. I would still feel upset. I would try to start a dialogue with the manager about where I can improve and provide concrete situations where I deserved positive feedback. Otherwise, I would request a review with a 3rd party.

I would reschedule if it isn't something urgent. If it is something urgent, then I will stand my ground. I get it done and out of the way, whether I have something last minute pop or not, I would only have to focus on that said project. I would get the higher up involved because I want to be heard and given a fair shot like everyone else.

I would see if my plans are flexible and if so, change them to accommodate my colleague. If my plans are flexible, it won't hurt to change them and help my colleague at the same time. I would try and finish the project as soon as possible so that I would have time for any last-minute tasks provided by my supervisor. I would try to arrange a meeting with my supervisor to discuss the feedback and also send an email to document my disagreement with the feedback.

I would agree to move to another week provided I can use one day's less leave than the period I have off i.e. I get a "free day".  I would do this to show willingness to be flexible, provided I get something in return. I would avoid my boss for the next two weeks.  This would minimise the chance of him giving me something which will take significant amounts of time and affection. I would be unhappy.  I would raise it with me manager's manager and, if that was unsuccessful, I would take it to my Trade Union.

I would change my week of vacation.  I would want to get along with everyone and I can be flexible in this situation. I would proactively get the project done ahead of time to prevent last minute work additions from interfering with its completion.  I would want to make sure I got it done on time. I would feel very disheartened over the situation.  I would go to my manager and ask for additional clarification and insight.  I would want to identify why I had gaps in my performance from his perspective.

I would do my best to change my plans and offer to change them for the colleague. I would look and see if I could make changes without it costing too much. I would finish the project ahead of time before the due date in case of receiving extra work near the deadline. I would ask my manager to elaborate and expand on the feedback so I could understand and improve and see their point of view even if I did not agree.

I would leave it up to the supervisor or manager to decide who gets the time off, based on who asked first, seniority, etc. This seems more fair and more like a decision that a manager should be making, rather than leaving it up to employees to decide on themselves. I'd get started on it as early as possible, just in case extra work comes up. I think this would set me up for success and would avoid stress. I would feel disappointed and like I was being treated unfairly. I would plead my case about the feedback to my manager and see if I could persuade them to see things from my side.

I'm happy to change my schedule if it's more convenient for the schedule. If my colleague already requested the week off first, and they already have plans in place, I wouldn't want to be the one to spoil their fun. I would still start the project right away and finish it as soon as I can, provided I have the time to. That way, I can use my free time to help out with other tasks or assist my colleagues in other areas, should they need an extra hand. I would reflect on their feedback seriously and see which areas I would need to improve in order to meet their expectations and standards. I set high standards for myself, and I would hope that others see my effort in the same light.

No problem. I'm flexible. Please let me know when I have some open weeks so I can set up my time off. I game for whatever. I would finish the task as soon as possible Then, I would assess the situation and ask, at the right time and under the right circumstances, if I can take on extra work. While the initial shock of negative feedback is always unpleasant, I would try to understand the reasons for it and adjust to make the organization, and my work, better.

If I found myself in the situation, my priority would be to ensure that our team's work and the care we provide to our clients remains uninterrupted. Since my colleague is unwilling to change their plans, I'll be happy to look at my own schedule to see if there is another week that could work well for me.  

I believe that fostering a supportive and cooperative team environment is essential for us to be effective at all of our jobs. If moving my vacation helps the team manage to work a little more smoothly during that busy period, then I am more than willing to be flexible. My goal was to make sure that our office runs well so we can continue providing the best possible support to the families and individuals who truly rely on this. By remaining adaptable, I can help resolve this conflict in a way that respects my colleagues needs and ensures our professional responsibilities are fully covered. To make sure that the project is completed successfully, I will prioritize getting the core components finished well ahead of the deadline rather than waiting until the last minute. This proactive approach would allow me to remain fully supportive so I can help my supervisor and colleagues if unexpected or urgent tasks arise. Since I understand that our work environment can be unpredictable, managing my own workload efficiently is my way of being a truly cooperative team member. By staying ahead of my own deadlines, I can confidently shift my focus up with new priorities without feeling overly stressed or compromising the quality of our service. Ultimately, my goal is to remain a reliable, helpful presence for the team so that our clients needs are always met, regardless of how busy things get. I believe that receiving feedback like this would be extremely disappointing, especially when I feel it doesn't accurately reflect my hard work and real dedication to my clients. However, I recognize that my managers perspective is valuable, and I would approach the situation with the sincere desire to understand their concerns rather than getting mad or defensive. I will schedule a calm, private follow - up meeting to express my thoughts respectfully and ask for specific examples that led to their evaluation. By listening actively and sharing my own side, hope we can find common ground and clarify any misunderstandings. My main priority remains maintaining a positive, professional relationship with my manager so we can keep effectively supporting those that we serve. Ultimately, I believe that open and honest communication is the best way to move forward and ensure I am meeting the team's expectations.

I would happily change my vacation plans. If they can't change their plans it must be important. I would want to help. I would get the first project done as quickly as possible. I would make sure I had the first project finished even if additional tasks were added. I would talk with the supervisor and try to figure out what I need to change. I would try to get better feedback in the future.

I would want to know why the collegue is unwilling to change their plans and what they plan on doing. Maybe they are going on a family trip that cannot be rescheduled. If that wqas the case then I would be fine with allowing them to have that week. I would work extra hard to get the project done as quickly as possible. That way, if I do get a last minute project it wouldn't create undue burden on myself or the organization. I would try to talk with the manager to see why they feel this way. Maybe I can convince them that the feedback is unwarranted. I could try to show them that they are wrong about the feedback.

I would see if I can change my vacation days, but at the same time try to get an extra PTO or something to make my sacrifice worth it. I would try to tackle the project as soon as possible just in case there are unexpected tasks that will come up to change my workload. I would ask the manager for a quick one on one and ask in detail why they provided the feedback. I would try to understand their perspective and see if I'm just not seeing what they are seeing.

Well, how far in advance are we asking for our vacations? Has either one of us bought anything for it? I would base it off of who asked for vacation first at that point and how far in advance gets to go on vacation. If it's a small company I may need to understand that I have to change what week my vacation is, if it's a large company with plenty of staff then I think it's up to management to find coverage for that weekend/week if I am appropriately asking far enough in advance i.e. 6 months or so for this vacation. I would do all of my project everyday/work on it as soon as possible to be done as soon as possible to prep for last minute tasks. Once I finish my task too, I would ask my manager if they have anything else available that they would want me to do, right now or soon so I can begin working on it. I would ask why they feel that negative feedback, it really depends on what it is affecting my annual bonus and if it's subjective. Is it my attitude? I should probably change my attitude then. Is it my attendance? Is there a reason for my poor attendance? Is it my work and the quality of work I'm committing to? I think to take money away from me it is kind of important for them to give a reason on the negative feedback and what is it.

I would honestly ask which one of us submitted time off/vacation first and have that granted. Otherwise, I would see if I could swap my vacation for another week and work my way around it if that's the case. I would attend to the project carefully and thoroughly. I would also give myself time to receive more last tasks from my boss especially knowing that it will take significant amounts of time and most likely will allocate the time for it. I would feel devastated and upset about the feedback and situation. I would discuss with my boss about what can be done to improve and to ask for the chance to improve in order to avoid any bad consequences. People should be given the chance to fix situations in the work place and improve, but they should also be supported in doing so by their boss.

I would proceed with moving my vacation to a different day, since it seems like my colleague cannot reschedule their days off. I would begin working on this project as early as possible, in order to get ahead of any potential issues. I would feel a bit down on myself. I would probably reach out to my manager and ask for specifics about the negative feedback, as well as how to improve.

i would let them have the week off and i would choose another week. its important to be flexible and get along with coworkers and protect the mission i would resolve it right away, to ensure i was available to do anything else needed. i would even volunteer for more work once done i would ask to speak with them and find out how i can improve and be better, apologize and ask for a second chance with them

Assuming my vacation did not impact something significant i.e. an anniversary, I would be willing to reschedule my vacation week to one of my choosing. If it did impact a significant event, I would go by seniority rules or whoever submitted first. I would complete it as early as possible, and while not outright telling my boss I had finished it, I would continue to touch base to see if these last minute tasks are likely to appear. I would give myself a grace period with the last few days to get it done, if possible. Same as the previous answer to this question. I would dig further into where the negative feedback came from, and if it was overblown or misconstrued. I would highlight the positive effect my work has on the team. I would still legally document everything as a precaution.

I am typically very flexible in scenarios such as this. Since this scenario describes going on vacation and not needing urgent time off for an emergency, I would figure out another time that would be suitable for me to go on vacation. The reason I would do this is because I am a team player and I have no issues with compromising so that everyone ends up happy. If I am aware that my boss sometimes gives last-minute tasks, then I would manage my time accordingly. I would make a list of what work I have left to complete, and I would allot extra time into my schedule for unforeseen tasks. I would do this because it would help me prioritize. I would accept the feedback graciously. However, if I disagreed with it, I would ask the manager for clarification on why he felt this particular feedback was appropriate. I would inform the manager that my intentions are to be clear on what was unsatisfactory about my performance so that I can avoid repeating the same mistakes in the future.

I would request vacation for a different week because I don't have any definite travel plans booked.  If I did though, I would need to talk with my colleague and explore the situation and attempt to work out some type of compromise.  If this colleague is totally unwilling to change his/her vacation plans, and he/she does have a good reason not to, I would be understanding of the situation and change my vacation plans. Even though my workload is light, I would increase my work ability in those weeks leading up to the due date of the project.  Thus I would be completing the project as planned as I want to be able to work steadily and at a reasonable pace rather than get stressed and possibly make mistakes attempting to complete the project. I would need further clarification as to why I have received negative feedback and ask the manager if there is some way to counteract that negativity.  If there is some type of work that I can do, or change my work behaviour so that I am still able to gain my annual bonus.

I would change my vacation date.  Everyone appreicates a co worker that can be flexible and easy to work with, and there really is not much difference in weeks anyway. I would complete it early so that I would have time to edit it.  This would also give me additional time to make additions or complete additional tasks (if my boss needs this done). I would take some time to reflect on the feedback.  If after careful review I still disagree objectively, then I would follow the directions and feedback for now (as long as I am not being asked to do anything against my values or morales).  However, I would be looking for another job because it is important to be working with good people that support you.  Otherwise, you are stressed out all the time and not getting raises or bonuses that you deserve.

I would discuss with my coworker about the situation and see what the vacation is for, and kindly explain my situation as well. I would also speak to my supervisor about how to navigate it. I would take extra time to do a little more work to make sure that the project is prepared and ready early just in case. I would find proof that shows that it is not true, and speak to hr or another supervisor about the situation to make sure it gets resolved.

I would see who can cancel the trip and get their money back. if neither one of us can then I would see who got approved first. Whoever got approved first should go. I would do the project to the best of my ability and ask for help if i need it. Sometimes you just need to ask for help if you need it. I would ask them about it and see why they gave me that feedback. I would also ask coworker if they agree with it. If it is truly unfair even asking discussing with everyone, I would go over my managers head, but only if it is 100 percent wrong feedback and talking to my manager first

I would go ahead and accept working the week since it would be too inconvenient for the workplace and the people that could need me. I would make sure to make good progress on the project to leave my schedule as open as I can near the due date to prepare for anything unexpected. I would discuss the situation more deeply with the manager while also taking the feedback into consideration even if I don't agree with it.

I would ask for a compromise in this situation. I would ask them if they could do vacation another week etc. I would get the project done earlier so I could do whatever in the future. If it's too much, I would ask my boss for more time to get it done. I would just ignore it for the most part. I would feel bad about the situation, but I wouldn't let me stop working to the best of my ability.

I would change my vacation week providing that i did not have something planned that required me to forfeit my deposit or incur huge cancellation fees. I would have to bring my work home. The projects given to me by my boss would be priority during regular work hours, and i would try to work on the other project after hours, as overtime or work from home project. I would request a meeting with a higher up, a supervisor or even a boss, if i was 100% sure that my manager is in the wrong. I would not be able to live with the injustice and perform my job correctly.

I would change my plans in order to make sure work continues to function smoothly and that my coworkers and clients are taken care of. I would work ahead as much as possible, and be proactive by asking my boss ahead of time if there are any additional tasks that will need to be done. I would look over my work and reflect on the manager's words, and if I still felt that they were incorrect, I would ask to meet with them and ask for more clarification.

If I was able to arrange it, I would do my best to pick the different week to show that I'm a team player. I would get started on the project ahead of time and try to finish early in case other tasks come up. I would ask to schedule a meeting with the manager and state my evidence on why I don't feel the feedback is accurate.  I would calmly ask them how they came to that conclusion.

I would change my vacation plans. I wouldn't want to cause drama over this. Especially if I have to see my colleague everyday. I would stay calm and write tasks in my planner to what I have to do everyday. As my boss gives me tasks I can add it to my planner to see what/how I can get them done. I would feel a little disappointed in myself. I would keep calm and try to use their negative feedback to improve my work.

I would try to negotiate so that if I give in this time, I could have first pick of vacation next time. Also, I think it would be worth talking to the supervisor to see who put in their vacation plan notice first. I would start work right away so that I don't have to stress later. I don't want to wait and then be rushed later. I would try to document my work so that I could avoid negative consequences in the future and start looking for a new job.

I depends on the type of plans I've made. If I planned to spend the vacation week by myself, I'd be open to postponing mine. But if I made plans with someone else, I will also insist on having my vacation that week. I'm a chronic procrastinator at my core, and I'm well aware of Parkinson's law. So when I have a tsk, I try to finish it as early as possible. Then take the rest of the time improving on it. I would speak up, and give him reasons why i disagree with his feedback. I typically don't like confrontation, but if it carries heavy consequences, I simply cannot abide.

I would try to understand and have a conversation with my colleague on why that certain week is important to her, and try to compromise. If I'm able to change my schedule, I would just ask for another week off, but if not, I would ask for the supervisor's help. I would handle this by asking my boss for more time, considering it was given to me at the last minute. If not, I would do as asked buf if it is a common occurence I would take matters with the higher-ups. I would have a conversation with the manager and try to understand his point of view and why he had negative feedback on my work. If the conversation doesn't go to would rather just redo it to their liking rather than risking my bonus.

I would give up my plans and let them take their vacation.  I don't want to have a conflict and play the peacemaker. I would get the project done as soon as possible.  I would like to get  it out of the way. It would be good for my mental health. I  would  calmly talk to my boss and present evidence of my great work and I would also speak calmly and avoid conflict.

I would probably end up not taking the vacation so I can keep the peace and to avoid any further conflict between us. I would work ahead in the anticipation that I will get a project assigned to me, just so I can get the first project done. I would be annoyed and probably try to set up a meeting to push back or request documentation or something to leave a paper trail.

If I possible could, I would change my plans to go a different week so we wouldn't be shorthanded. If that is not possible, I would talk with my colleague and try to get a sense of any other way we could solve this problem. I would check in with my boss early, like now, to see if she is thinking of anything else that should be added to the project. I would clarify with her where the project stands right now and let her know that I am confident that the project will be done on time unless the scope changes in the next two weeks. I would take a day or two to think it over. Then I would ask to meet with my manager again to talk more about the feedback and why I believe it is inaccurate. I would bring specific examples with me to talk about. I would also be very clear with her that I heard the feedback and am going to take steps to improve my performance.

I would go ahead and change my plans because not only would it make me look good with the organization, cooperating, but it will also be a good way to have him owe me for something in the future. I never leave things till the last minute, and I always get things done early, this project would not be different. I will get it down as best, and as fast as I can, but I'm ready for anything extra that might come along. I would meet with my manager one on one and try to figure out what went wrong, then why he or she has an accurate information about me, if we can iron it out I will stick up for myself and go around him to the next immediate supervisor.

I would try to speak with my colleague and compare our schedules. If the colleague had flights or hotels that could not be cancelled, then I would seriously consider changing my plans. If we did, I would maybe ask for a day off when they returned. I would use the extra time I currently have and go ahead and do my best to complete the project. I have always felt that even though a project may not be due right away it is always best to finish it as early as possible. This leaves time to look over and revise if needed. I would request another meeting with my manager. Before this meeting, I would look back over the past couple of months and make sure that I am unable to identify the issues that caused the negative feedback. If the issues were warranted, then I would do what I needed to do to fix them.

Again I would try to come to an understanding with my colleague about who would change their plans. If that didn't work I guess I would leave up to our supervisor. This could also be a family decision and not just up to us. I would strive to get the project ready before the two weeks is up. I would also work on it on my time if possible. I would probably just agree with the manager so I didn't loose my bonus or worse. I wouldn't be happy if I lost my bonus and still had to work hard .

I would discuss the situation with my colleague until we find a solution. It is important for my to remain open minded and respectful while having the conversation. I would start this project ahead of time. In doing so, I would be able to carefully assess what I have to do properly and accurately. This would help me stay organized and on time when submitting projects. I would feel disappointed but be understanding. I would be open to feedback and take note on what my manger asks me to change. From there, I would do the best I can to satisfy my manager.

As long as they requested the time off first, I would not make an issue of it. I would gladly take time off some other time. I would work ahead and make sure my project is completed at least a few days ahead of time, so I can be prepared for additional tasks. I would be calm and open in my demeanor. I might ask questions or display examples of performance that contradict his or her viewpoint.

If I was able to, I would change my vacation plans. I am willing to be a team player and make sacrifices for others. I would start the project right away so that I can finish it while my workload is light.  In case another project comes up later, I will have already got a good start on this one. I would try and have a discussion with the manager to see where they are coming from. I would also try to make my side heard and see if there is a way to meet in the middle.

I would postpone my vacation plans and instead show up to work that week. My needs are not time sensitive and I am able to delay them. I want to show myself as a team player and my attendance will ensure things remain operational. I would try to get most of the project done as soon as possible. It doesn't seem like it would be too involved, but you want to give yourself a buffer in case unexpected delays come up. Also, things don't always go as predicted. It's better to stay ahead of the eight ball so you can adjust to any roadblocks. We might underestimate the scope of the project and there will need to be adjustments. Staying ahead lets me account for these issues when they arise. I would want to communicate with them so I could better understand their position. I would politely ask to meet with them so we could discuss the issue. I would mention that I care about my performance and want to know how it can be improved. By elaboration, I can better understand their perspective and it will help my quality. There is always a chance that we can make amends and see eye to eye after this.

I would let my supervisor know that my plans are flexible if my colleague also wants to go that week.
