If other people need my help with something during this period, I could take time out to help them. In this case, I would ask for my supervisor to have a follow-up meeting in a few weeks, specifically addressing the items they did not like. I would plan to ask for feedback again to see if I have improved during that time period.

I would be willing to let my colleague have the vacation for the week they want. I would change my vacation plans to a different week. I am willing to do this because it is important for me to cooperate with my colleagues and to have a healthy work environment. I want the best for everyone, even if it means sacrificing myself. It is important to me to place the needs of others first and maintain peace and harmony by not being selfish. I would handle this project by making sure it gets done as soon as possible. Knowing that my boss gives me last-minute tasks means I need to get my projects done first to make sure I have enough time if my boss gives me other tasks. Doing this project early means freeing up my schedule and managing my time efficiently, so I'm not stressed if a time-consuming tasks comes up. I want to be sure all projects and tasks are done well and do not conflict with each other. I would feel upset because it seems unfair that the feedback is not true at all. I would feel uncomfortable, but I would try to talk to my manager to handle this. It is unhealthy to have resentment or festering feelings in the workplace. I would try to communicate with my manager to understand the opposing perspectives and come to an understanding. This is something that can be resolved, and we could have a healthy discussion so that I can better understand what my manager expects of me. My manager could also understand my perspective and intentions, and that could lead to an improvement for all parties.

I would let them have it and reschedule my vacation. I don't feel like arguing and I feel like their vacation maybe more important than mine. I would ask the boss what else needs done and help whoever needs help to further the project. I like to help everyone out and work together to finish the project. I'm not sure I deserve the negative feedback, but if my boss thinks it's accurate, I will accept the situation. I would just try and do my best next time.

I would speak to the colleague to see if there's any work around to make it work. If not, then I would be happy to change my vacation time. I would rather keep a good relationship with that coworker then make them angry. I would complete the project now. I would also ask my boss, once I've complete the first project, if there's anything they need help with. That way I can get on top of anything that may land on my desk with only a short amount of time to complete. I would feel hurt about the negative feedback but I think feedback helps people become better employees. So I would ask how I can improve and not make the same mistakes in the future.

I would ask the supervisor if they can accept the vacation time for the person that asked for that time off first. I would make sure to use any free time on the project and make that project a priority, especially the closer to the due date. I would ask a neutral co-worker and/or manager what their thoughts were on the feedback. If they agreed with me, I wouldn't take my manager's feedback too seriously.

I would tell my coworker that they are free to take that week of vacation. I would simply work that week in order to make sure my company is still in good standing. If something comes up around here, I want to be known as the one who makes things alright. If my coworker refuses to come in and work, I will gladly come in and pick up the slack for the sake of the company. I would simply stay focused on completing all my work in the promised timeframe. I would make sure to prioritize this project and my organizational skills. I will do this to ensure all requirements from me are met and no one says anything negatively about my performance. I feel determined about this situation. I would focus on the feedback and find any way I possibly could to apply it to make my job situation positive again. I would not take it personally and remember that I am there to do my best work.

I would see who has the most seniority between us. Seniority can be a fair way to decide who gets that certain vacation week. I would talk to my supervisor about how I am feeling and the concerns that I have. Based on what my supervisor says, I would go from there with a strategic plan. I would immediately document the negative feedback. I would probably contradict each line of negative feedback with a rebuttal and/or concern. I would also start journaling every positive thing that happens while I am at work. I would build an armor of evidence against the supervisor's negativity.

I would find out who submitted their vacation request first and determine if I needed to change or hold fast. I would get the project done, review with any stakeholders and confirm there are no last minute changes and then submit the project.  If there are some changes needed, I would then have time to update the project.
 It depends upon the facts of the feedback and what has supported that feedback.  Perceptions carry little weight when not backed by facts, negative or positive.

I would try to rearrange my vacation plans to fit into the work schedule my manager has set for one of us to do I would start early so I'm prepared for any sudden increases in work load and also so that I'm ahead and don't risk falling behind I would ask my manager to provide more detail in the feedback and highlight what I specifically need to improve on

I would bring forward or delay my vacation to accommodate them, assuming i've not booked any accommodation or flights that can't be changed.
This should show that i'm flexible and willing to compromise. I would keep my boss updated with my progress and on many occasions see if i'm able to draw any further tasks out of him. I'd be upset but accept that that's the nature and risk of having a job, and that i'm not the only one this happens to.
I'd take steps to show them that i'm working on that aspect, even if in reality i'm doing no different from what i was doing in the past.

I would change my date, just to be friendly to my colleague.
I can always take a vacation at a time more suitable to everyone involved. I would still try to ensure that my workload is actioned ahead of time.

Talk to colleague to see if they are able to reconsider, and also compare holiday plans before each of us. See if I could renegotiate my vacation plans so that we can all get a week off as needed, as long as my plans aren't already set in stone. I would change my vacation plans in order to help out my coworker, since they aren't willing to change. I would change my vacation plans with my colleague isn't willing to change theirs. I would do this because it would be the best way to avoid conflict with my colleague. I would be willing to change my vacation so everyone is able to get along and to reduce any conflict. Once again, I would let them go. I don't know if my plans are non negotioable but like whatever. I give up. It's not worth the fight I would! I am able to take on multiple projects at once. I decided to propose to my colleague if they feel this is the only time they can go, then i wouldnt mind going when they got back.  I would try to learn more about the feedback and steps I could take in the future to avoid negative comments in the past. Every experience can be a learning opportunity, so I could use this as a chance to improve.

I consult my colleague and discuss the situation incase he is not willing to change his vacation week i would change my vacation week and allow my colleague go first because it is too busy at work and one person has to stay. i would switch my vacation to ensure smooth operations at work, Mov ing forward i would cordinate with my coworker.  I would complete my workload as early on as possible and ask my boss if he has any other tasks to give me that may take a while. would not have a problem changing my plans and move the date to accommodate the work load so that all work is covered by someone. It depends if my timeframe was critical, but if it wasn’t I’d just take a different week I guess if I couldn’t get my coworker to change their mind because I don’t really have any choice I’d start it earlier than I anticipated to ensure that I get the project done of course despite thinking that I could knock it out the park just to be sure Take the feedback and tell my coworker to shove it up their ass, no I’m just kidding, I’d take the feedback and try to apply it and be better I guess

 I would take advantage of the light workload and go ahead and get the project completed just in case my boss sent another project during the same time.  I would feel that i need to know why the feedback is bad. I would have a constructive meeting to have an explanation and how to go moving forward.
